content.problemStatement:
You're given a binary string SSS, i.e. a string consisting of only the characters 000 and 111.

You can modify it by performing the following operation:


Choose an index iii (1≤i≤N−31 \leq i \leq N-31≤i≤N−3) such that Si=Si+2S_i = S_{i+2}Si​=Si+2​, Si+1=Si+3S_{i+1} = S_{i+3}Si+1​=Si+3​, and Si≠Si+1S_i \neq S_{i+1}Si​=Si+1​.

Then, swap Si+1S_{i+1}Si+1​ and Si+2S_{i+2}Si+2​.
Or, more simply, you can choose a substring equal to 0101\text{0101}0101 and make it 0011\text{0011}0011, or choose a substring equal to 1010\text{1010}1010 and make it 1100\text{1100}1100.

Count the number of distinct binary strings you can make by performing this operation several (possibly, zero) times.

The answer can be large, so print it modulo 998244353998244353998244353.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the length of the string.
The second line contains the binary string SSS.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the number of distinct binary strings that can be formed,  modulo 998244353998244353998244353.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.
